usage: Auto-backend hardware detection — bloodhound sniffing out GPU/CPU capabilities
concept: Bloodhound tracking scent trails that dissolve into hardware detection data flows. Nose becomes a sensor probe, scent particles become GPU/RAM/disk data points, ground becomes a decision tree that branches to different model tiers.
prompt: A striking 18th century natural history engraving of a large bloodhound (Canis lupus familiaris, St. Hubert variety) with its nose pressed intently to the ground, tracking a scent trail, rendered in extremely fine black ink cross-hatching on pure white background, 16:9 landscape format. The bloodhound's magnificent drooping ears and wrinkled skin are rendered in full classical engraving precision with dense parallel hatching. Its nose and muzzle gradually dissolve into a precision sensor probe — the folds of skin becoming concentric scanning rings. The scent particles rising from the ground transform into tiny data point symbols: miniature GPU chips, memory module rectangles, and disk platters floating upward in a scent-like trail pattern. The ground beneath the bloodhound's paws dissolves from natural earth texture into a clean binary decision tree diagram: branches splitting at diamond-shaped decision nodes labeled with conditions like greater-than symbols and threshold numbers, each terminal leaf containing a miniature model name. The bloodhound's muscular body is two-thirds pure biological engraving, with the technical dissolution concentrated in the nose area and the ground. The tracking posture conveys intense focused detection — this creature FINDS what you need. A few small labeled boxes float in the background like specimen tags: 48GB, 24GB, 8GB, CPU — representing the hardware tiers being detected. The dissolution is seamless with identical line weight throughout. Pure black ink on absolute white background. No color. No sepia. No cream. Stark contrast. Museum-quality Buffon-style natural history illustration meets hardware specification diagram.
